title: Lira Pressure, a New Party, and a Tense Strait
overview: Regional tensions over the Strait of Hormuz continue to weigh on the lira and Turkish bond markets even as Türkiye stays largely on the sidelines of the conflict itself. At home, the biggest domestic story is the launch of a new opposition party, while Ankara's F-35 ambitions hit another wall in Washington.

sections.heading: Money: Lira Under Renewed Pressure
sections.body: The Central Bank is expected to hold rates steady as the Middle East conflict complicates the disinflation path, with USD/TRY now at 47.2. Fitch says Türkiye needs sustained reserve buildup for a rating upgrade, while Citigroup has cut its Turkish bond exposure on oil-price risk. For residents, the practical takeaway: expect continued lira softness and plan currency conversions accordingly. Separately, BKM data show Turkish credit card spending abroad hit a record 93.1 billion lira in May, up 67% year-on-year — a sign locals are increasingly hedging by spending internationally.

sections.heading: Hormuz Tensions: Steady, Not Alarming, for Türkiye
sections.body: Trump has warned the US will strike Iranian bridges or power plants for each attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, and four US service members were killed in recent Iran-linked attacks since the ceasefire ended. Iran has warned any strike on its infrastructure will bring a 'powerful' response. Türkiye's own travel advisory remains unchanged at Level 2 — this is a regional story affecting Gulf shipping and Iranian airspace, not day-to-day life in Türkiye, though residents should expect continued market spillover and possibly fluctuating flight routings near Iranian airspace.

sections.heading: Turkish Politics: A New Opposition Party
sections.body: Ousted opposition figure Özgür Özel has launched a new party aimed at challenging President Erdoğan. International outlets (DW) frame this as a direct challenge to the ruling party; state-aligned coverage of the launch itself is notably thin so far. Residents watching domestic politics should expect this to dominate opposition-leaning coverage in coming weeks.

sections.heading: F-35 and Defense Ties
sections.body: The US State Department has told Congress that Türkiye does not currently meet the legal conditions to rejoin the F-35 program — a reminder that defense-industry normalization remains stalled even as Washington reportedly moves toward a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, drawing analysis (Haaretz) on how these moves might unsettle Israel.

sections.heading: Migration Notes
sections.body: Two deportation cases are drawing attention this week: a Uyghur man reportedly deported from Germany to China, and a Turkish man deported from the US despite prior torture-related protections. Both stories illustrate the uneven and sometimes opaque nature of deportation decisions — worth noting for anyone navigating cross-border status issues.

closing: Keep an eye on the exchange rate today, and stay well — iyi günler.
